title: Abe Foxman, US Jewish leader, dies at 86
body:
The global Jewish community is mourning the loss of a prominent leader and steadfast advocate for Israel, Abraham Foxman, who passed away at the age of 86. For nearly three decades, Foxman served as the national director of the Anti-Defamation League, a prominent advocacy group dedicated to combating antisemitism and promoting social justice. During his tenure, Foxman became a household name, earning respect and admiration from world leaders, Jewish communities, and human rights organizations alike. His tireless efforts to promote tolerance, understanding, and support for the state of Israel have left an indelible mark on the global landscape.

Foxman's legacy is a testament to his unwavering commitment to the Jewish community and his unrelenting passion for social justice. Under his leadership, the Anti-Defamation League expanded its reach and influence, becoming a leading voice in the fight against hate and bigotry. Foxman was a vocal critic of antisemitism in all its forms, from the streets of Europe to the halls of academia. He worked tirelessly to promote Holocaust education, combat hate speech, and support the rights of Jewish communities around the world. His dedication to the cause earned him numerous accolades, including the prestigious Israel Prize, the country's highest civilian honor.

Israeli officials have hailed Foxman as a "warm and passionate" advocate, whose love for the Jewish state was only matched by his commitment to its people. Foxman was a frequent visitor to Israel, where he worked closely with government leaders, civil society organizations, and community groups to promote mutual understanding and cooperation. His efforts helped to strengthen ties between Israel and the global Jewish diaspora, fostering a sense of unity and purpose among Jewish communities worldwide. Foxman's passing has sent shockwaves throughout the Jewish world, with leaders and ordinary citizens alike paying tribute to his memory and legacy.

Beyond his work with the Anti-Defamation League, Foxman was a respected voice on issues of social justice and human rights. He was a vocal advocate for the rights of marginalized communities, from African Americans to LGBTQ+ individuals, and worked to promote greater understanding and empathy between different faith and cultural groups. Foxman's commitment to social justice was rooted in his own experiences as a Holocaust survivor, having been hidden as a child by a Polish Catholic family during World War II. This personal history informed his lifelong dedication to fighting hate and promoting tolerance, inspiring countless others to follow in his footsteps.

As the Jewish community mourns the loss of a beloved leader, it is also reflecting on the challenges that lie ahead. The fight against antisemitism and hate remains an urgent and ongoing concern, with rising tensions and violent attacks targeting Jewish communities around the world. Foxman's passing serves as a reminder of the importance of continued vigilance and activism, as well as the need for greater cooperation and understanding between different faith and cultural groups. As the world looks to the future, it is clear that Foxman's legacy will continue to inspire and guide efforts to promote social justice, combat hate, and support the Jewish community.

In the days and weeks ahead, the global Jewish community will come together to celebrate Foxman's life and legacy, even as it grapples with the challenges of a rapidly changing world. As we look to the future, it is clear that Foxman's passing marks the end of an era, but also presents an opportunity for new leaders and advocates to emerge. The fight against hate and intolerance is far from over, but with Foxman's legacy as a guiding light, there is hope that a brighter, more just future is within reach. As the Jewish community says goodbye to a beloved leader, it is also looking to the future, determined to carry on Foxman's work and build a world that is more just, compassionate, and free from hate.
